title: Materialized cross-process query snapshots
description: Preserve stable pages and full-set totals across separate CLI invocations.
decision: The first actuals page validates input, acquires the single-writer lock, and runs one SQLite BEGIN IMMEDIATE transaction. Inside that transaction it resolves active financial state, computes the complete ordered projection and full-set exact totals, and inserts ephemeral QuerySnapshot and QuerySnapshotItem rows. It commits before returning an opaque random cursor and expiry. Busy before transaction acquisition returns LEDGER-SNAPSHOT-BUSY; rollback, cancellation, or crash before commit returns no cursor and leaves no partial snapshot. Later pages accept only the cursor and use read-only connections to fixed ordinals. Cursor version, contract version, filter hash, expiry, and store-generation fingerprint are validated. Cleanup is opportunistic; snapshots are excluded from backup and Durable Ledger State and do not consume mutation idempotency keys.
rationale: A SQLite read transaction cannot persist a snapshot and cannot span CLI processes. A separate read followed by write would race with another mutation. One BEGIN IMMEDIATE transaction serializes the coherent read, exact calculation, and ephemeral materialization under the supported single-writer model. Persisting only IDs would still allow later pages to change after corrections; persisting the returned projection and totals freezes the economic result at personal scale.
alternatives: A: offset pagination over live rows — rejected as unstable. B: cursor holding only last sort key — rejected after writes. C: long-running daemon transaction — prohibited. D: read transaction followed by snapshot write — rejected because state can change between phases. E: one BEGIN IMMEDIATE transaction that reads, computes, and materializes — chosen.
consequences: The first actuals page briefly takes the writer lock and may return LEDGER-SNAPSHOT-BUSY under contention; later pages are read-only. Snapshots consume temporary local space and expire after a documented window. No cursor is emitted until commit. Changed filters with a cursor are schema errors. Ordering is effective-date descending then transaction ULID descending. Expired, missing, incompatible, or stale-generation cursors return stable errors.
